body: "1. Introductions  This is a summary of the discussions from the Strategic Public Engagement Group (SPEG) meeting held on Tuesday 23 September 2025.  The meeting was co-chaired by The Hon. Mr. Justice Robin Knowles CBE and Miriam Davidson (Head of User Inclusion, HMCTS).  2. Overarching Evaluation of HMCTS Reform  Members received an update on the MOJ’s overarching evaluation of HMCTS reform, following an earlier presentation to the group.  Discussion highlighted how a theory-based impact evaluation approach will assess whether and how reform has met its aims focusing on access to justice and using MI data, evidence reviews, research studies and project-level evaluations.  Reports on each area will be published on Gov.UK as they are completed.  The final MOJ report is due in Spring 2027.   3. Neurodivergence – a strategic issue for the justice system  Autism Alliance UK led members in a discussion on strategic considerations for the justice system to ensure the needs of neurodivergent users are reflected and met in the design and delivery of its services and access to justice.   Autism Alliance UK highlighted how neurodivergent conditions such as ADHD, autism, dyslexia and dyspraxia impact users in different ways and outlined how HMCTS should consider a strength-based approach to service design that focuses on the strengths of neurodivergent people and not just their challenges, which will help develop more inclusive and efficient processes and ultimately benefit all users.  Further discussion focused on the different forms of guidance, support and communications neurodivergent users typically need to help them navigate the justice system, including having a range of online resources, simplified court communications, pre-hearing familiarisation visits and clear signposting to other available support.  Incorporating lived experience was also considered essential to increase understanding and improve services for neurodivergent users, requiring continued collaboration between HMCTS and the third sector to ensure a focus on user needs and outcomes.  Further engagement will be planned for future SPEG meetings.   4. Remote Participation Approach and Strategy  HMCTS outlined the Remote Participation Approach and sought feedback from the group to inform development of HMCTS’ Remote Participation Strategy, which aims to help deliver access to justice by continually improving the remote hearings process for users and reducing the overall case backlog.  It was highlighted that approximately 70% of court buildings are equipped for remote hearings and currently around 25% of all hearings are held remotely.  The group discussed how remote hearings could be made more effective and accessible for users and how their needs are considered.  Measures identified included a more standardised approach to remote hearings across the different jurisdictions, simplified and more timely communications from courts when sending electronic links to users for their hearing, and supporting vulnerable users who may lack adequate private space by utilising sites such as law centres or other government buildings.  HMCTS were also encouraged to ensure that the strategy takes account of findings from existing research on digital disadvantage.     Further discussion highlighted types of hearing considered suitable for remote participation, for example online civil money claims, and focused on challenges such as ensuring a consistent approach to implementing support and special measures for vulnerable users, increasing user awareness of remote hearing options and expectations on etiquette, and ensuring user needs are identified as early as possible during their legal journey.   The Remote Participation Strategy is expected in the new year and will likely be discussed further at SPEG once it has been progressed.   5. Next meeting  The next meeting is planned for March 2026. "
